episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Tyee] A Deadly Fungal Invader Is Threatening BC’s Bats and [BC Gov News] Opening Clean Industry Fund 2026 intake, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Who controls ecological knowledge and data in Cariboo wilderness, and does technology democratization actually distribute power or concentrate it further? Topics include: Data sovereignty and Indigenous data governance frameworks (OCAP principles, UNDRIP implementation), Co-management models for monitoring infrastructure versus province-run systems, Citizen science limitations and strengths in low-population-density rural regions, Backcountry safety infrastructure (satellite communicators, SAR funding models), Maintenance and sustainability of remote monitoring equipment in harsh climates.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

discussion.central_question: Who controls ecological knowledge and data in Cariboo wilderness, and does technology democratization actually distribute power or concentrate it further?
discussion.riley_position: Democratized sensing technology and improved connectivity have genuinely distributed monitoring capacity, and co-management models with First Nations can create more reliable infrastructure than province-run systems if paired with genuine authority transfer and co-funding.
riley_key_evidence: Tŝilhqot'in Nation Forestry LP successfully integrates traditional ecological knowledge with satellite-based forest monitoring operationally
Blueberry River First Nations IPCA agreement established a model for Nation-centered data governance and monitoring frameworks
Satellite connectivity improvements like Starlink enable real-time data transmission from remote locations where it wasn't previously viable
discussion.casey_position: Technological democratization masks deeper governance and power asymmetries; data collection without corresponding decision-making authority and sustained funding creates the appearance of equity while offloading infrastructure costs onto under-resourced communities.
casey_key_evidence: Blueberry River monitoring framework only emerged after litigation forced the province to the table, not through proactive institutional design
University research teams deploying sensors on Secwépemc territory often retain data control in academic databases requiring formal Nation requests for access
Remote monitoring cameras receive deprioritized maintenance during budget cycles, systematically affecting highest-need remote locations
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey converge on identifying governance capacity and sustainable funding as the actual bottleneck (not technology), and both agree that genuine co-management requires pre-negotiated data sovereignty agreements, adequate co-funding, and community-led decision-making authority—with Casey emphasizing the need for operational OCAP principles and template agreements to prevent repeated extraction.
topics_covered: Data sovereignty and Indigenous data governance frameworks (OCAP principles, UNDRIP implementation)
Co-management models for monitoring infrastructure versus province-run systems
Citizen science limitations and strengths in low-population-density rural regions
Backcountry safety infrastructure (satellite communicators, SAR funding models)
Maintenance and sustainability of remote monitoring equipment in harsh climates
